input: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You have been strolling by the trolley track, and you can see the situation at a glance: The driver saw five men on the track ahead, he stamped on the brakes, the brakes failed, so he fainted. There is a spur of track leading off to the right. There is one track workman on that spur of track. He can no more get off the track in time than the five can, so you will kill him if you turn the trolley onto him. Imagine that the five on the straight track are thin, but thick enough so that although all five will be killed if the trolley goes straight, the bodies of the five will stop it, and it will therefore not reach the one. On the other hand, the one on the right-hand track is fat, so fat that his body will by itself stop the trolley, and the trolley will therefore not reach the five.  Should you turn the trolley?

[Deontologist]: From a deontological perspective, the question of whether to turn the trolley isn't about the numbers or the consequences. It's about the moral duty not to actively cause harm to an innocent individual. Turning the trolley would be an intentional act that results in the death of the one man on the spur. That action is inherently wrong, regardless of the outcome. It's our moral duty to uphold the principle that it's wrong to actively cause harm to an innocent person. So, no, I would not turn the trolley.

[Utilitarian]: I understand your perspective, focusing on moral duties and principles. However, from a utilitarian standpoint, the goal is to minimize total harm. In this scenario, one life is at stake if we turn the trolley, but five lives are at risk if we don't. The consequence of inaction is greater harm than the consequence of action. Isn't it our moral responsibility to act in a way that saves the most lives, even if it means making a difficult, active choice?

[Deontologist]: I see your point, but I must disagree. The utilitarian approach suggests that the ends justify the means, but from a deontological standpoint, certain actions are inherently wrong, regardless of their outcomes. By choosing to turn the trolley, we are actively making a decision that results in the death of an innocent person. That action violates the moral principle that it is wrong to actively cause harm to an innocent individual. It's not about the number of lives saved; it's about the moral duty to refrain from actively causing harm. Even if the consequence of inaction is greater harm, the moral principle remains unchanged. We should not compromise our moral duties for the sake of a more favorable outcome.

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your commitment to moral principles, but let's consider this: by not acting, we're still responsible for the outcome. The trolley will kill five people if we do nothing. Isn't our inaction still a choice, and thus, a decision that results in harm? From a utilitarian perspective, we have a responsibility to act in a way that minimizes harm, even if it means making a difficult decision. Can we truly uphold the principle that inaction is morally superior to action when the consequences of inaction are so severe?

[Deontologist]: I understand your argument, but I must stress that deontology distinguishes between action and inaction. Inaction, or allowing harm to occur, is not the same as actively causing harm. While it's true that not acting results in the deaths of five people, the moral principle is that we have a duty not to actively cause harm to an innocent individual. By choosing to turn the trolley, we are making an active decision that results in the death of one person. That action is inherently wrong, regardless of the consequences. The principle that it is wrong to actively cause harm to an innocent person is a moral duty that must be upheld, even if the outcome is not the most favorable. It's about the inherent wrongness of the action, not the consequences of that action.

[Utilitarian]: I see your perspective, but let's consider the trolley problem from a different angle. Imagine that instead of turning a trolley, you have the option to push a large person onto the tracks, stopping the trolley and saving the five. Would you do it? It's the same outcome, but the method is different. Does the principle hold if the action is more personal, more direct? How does that change your stance?

[Deontologist]: The scenario you've presented is indeed a different variation of the trolley problem, and it's an interesting one. From a deontological perspective, pushing a person onto the tracks to stop the trolley is still an active decision that results in the death of an innocent individual. The principle remains the same: it is wrong to actively cause harm to an innocent person. The method may be different, but the inherent wrongness of the action is unchanged. It's not about the physical act of turning a trolley versus pushing a person; it's about the moral duty not to actively cause harm to an innocent individual. So, no, I would not push the person onto the tracks, as it violates the moral principle that must be upheld.

[Utilitarian]: I see, so the principle is inviolable regardless of the method or the numbers involved. But let's think about this: if we have a moral duty not to actively cause harm, does that mean we also have a duty to intervene when we see harm about to occur? For example, if we see someone about to be harmed and we have the power to stop it without directly causing harm to another, shouldn't we intervene? Where do we draw the line between our duty not to cause harm and our duty to prevent it?

[Deontologist]: You raise an interesting point. From a deontological perspective, our duty is indeed to prevent harm, but not at the cost of actively causing harm to another innocent individual. If we can prevent harm without directly causing harm to someone else, then yes, we have a duty to intervene. For example, if we can warn the five men on the track to move out of the way without having to turn the trolley or push someone onto the tracks, then we should do so. The line is drawn at the point where preventing harm requires us to actively cause harm to an innocent person. Our duty is to prevent harm, but not by violating the moral principle that it is wrong to actively cause harm to an innocent individual.

[Utilitarian]: I think I understand your stance better now. However, I still find myself grappling with the idea that inaction in certain situations can lead to greater harm. Let me ask you this: if you were the trolley driver and you saw the five men on the track, would you feel any moral responsibility for their deaths if you did nothing? Even though you didn't actively cause their harm, your inaction led to their deaths. Doesn't that create a moral burden?

[Deontologist]: As the trolley driver, I would indeed feel a moral burden for the deaths of the five men if I did nothing. However, it's important to distinguish between the moral burden of inaction and the moral responsibility for actively causing harm. While I may feel guilty or responsible for their deaths due to my inaction, the moral principle remains that I have a duty not to actively cause harm to an innocent individual. The moral burden comes from the consequences of my inaction, but the moral responsibility for actively causing harm is a separate issue. I would not feel morally responsible for their deaths in the same way I would feel responsible if I had actively caused harm to someone else. It's a nuanced distinction, but an important one from a deontological perspective.

[Utilitarian]: I see your point about the distinction between moral burden and responsibility. However, I still struggle with the idea that the moral burden of inaction doesn't translate into moral responsibility. If we accept that our inaction can lead to harm, shouldn't we also accept that we have a responsibility to act when we can prevent greater harm? Even if it means making a difficult decision?

[Deontologist]: I understand your struggle, but I must firmly state that, from a deontological perspective, moral responsibility is tied to the active causing of harm. Inaction, while it may lead to harm, does not translate into the same moral responsibility. We have a duty to prevent harm, but not at the cost of violating the principle that it is wrong to actively cause harm to an innocent individual. The moral burden of inaction is a consequence of the situation, but it does not create a moral responsibility to act in a way that violates our moral duties. We must uphold the principle that certain actions are inherently wrong, regardless of the consequences.

[Utilitarian]: I respect your commitment to principle, but I must press further. What if the one man on the spur is a known murderer, guilty of heinous crimes? Would the moral calculus change if the one was a threat to society, and the five were innocent? Would it be morally permissible to turn the trolley then?

[Deontologist]: Even in this scenario, the moral principle remains unchanged. It is still wrong to actively cause harm to an individual, regardless of their past actions or the potential benefit to society. While it's true that the one man on the spur may pose a threat, the act of turning the trolley to kill him would still be an active decision to cause harm. From a deontological perspective, we must uphold the principle that it is wrong to actively cause harm to an innocent individual, and even if the one man is not innocent, the act of turning the trolley to kill him is still inherently wrong. We should not compromise our moral duties for the sake of a more favorable outcome, even if it means allowing harm to come to others.

[Utilitarian]: I see. It seems we're at an impasse, as our fundamental approaches to ethics differ. I appreciate our discussion and the chance to explore these complex moral dilemmas from different perspectives.
